title: Developer Tools Stack Guide
description: A practical walkthrough for assembling a modern development toolchain across version control, CI/CD, code quality, hosting, databases, and monitoring. This guide helps engineering teams evaluate and integrate the right tools at each layer of the stack.

sections.title: Version Control with GitHub
sections.body: GitHub remains the industry standard for Git-based version control, hosting over 100 million repositories. Use protected branches with required status checks to enforce code review discipline before any merge. Adopt a trunk-based development flow or GitHub Flow to keep mainline stable while enabling rapid feature work. Leverage GitHub Actions for lightweight automations directly inside your repository without third-party services.

sections.title: CI/CD Pipelines
sections.body: A robust CI/CD pipeline catches integration issues early and ships code reliably. Start by running linting, type-checking, and unit tests on every pull request. Add end-to-end tests for critical user flows in a staging environment before promoting to production. Consider tools like GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, or CircleCI — the key is keeping pipeline feedback under 10 minutes so developers stay in flow.

sections.title: Code Quality & Review
sections.body: Automate code quality enforcement with tools like ESLint, Prettier, and SonarQube integrated directly into your CI pipeline. Require at least one reviewer on every PR and use GitHub's code owners feature to route changes to the right domain experts. Set up automated dependency scanning with Dependabot to catch vulnerable packages before they reach production.

sections.title: Deployment & Hosting with Vercel
sections.body: Vercel provides edge-optimized hosting with automatic SSL, CDN distribution, and instant rollbacks. Connect your Git repository and every branch gets a preview URL — ideal for reviewing UI changes before merging. For static sites and frontend frameworks like Next.js, Vercel's incremental static regeneration eliminates build-time tradeoffs while keeping pages fresh.

sections.title: Database & Backend with Supabase
sections.body: Supabase offers a managed Postgres database with built-in authentication, real-time subscriptions, and auto-generated REST and GraphQL APIs. Use Row-Level Security policies to enforce per-user data access directly in the database layer, reducing backend boilerplate. For serverless workloads, pair Supabase with Edge Functions for low-latency API endpoints that scale to zero.

sections.title: Monitoring & Observability
sections.body: Instrument your stack with structured logging and distributed tracing to diagnose issues before users notice them. Tools like Datadog, Grafana, and Sentry provide dashboards for error tracking, latency breakdowns, and infrastructure health. Set up alerting on p99 latency and error budgets rather than raw CPU usage to align monitoring with user-facing reliability.

sections.title: Stack Evaluation Framework
sections.body: When evaluating a new developer tool for your stack, score it against four criteria: integration effort (hours to connect with existing tools), team adoption friction (learning curve for your least experienced engineer), maintenance burden (upgrade frequency, breaking changes per year), and exit cost (effort to migrate data and workflows to an alternative). A tool scoring below 7 out of 10 on any single criterion needs strong compensating advantages in the others. Prioritize tools with well-documented APIs and public roadmaps — these signals correlate with lower long-term maintenance burden. Start any new tool with a two-week evaluation period where your team uses it on a real but non-critical project before committing to a paid plan.

author: StackPilot Team
